title: What is an Operating System?
body: An OS manages hardware and software resources and provides a convenient environment to run programs by hiding hardware complexity. It acts as a resource manager.

title: Why do we need an OS?
body: List three problems if there were no OS: bulky apps with hardware code, resource exploitation by one app, and no memory protection.

title: OS goals and resource management
body: Name two classic OS goals (e.g. maximum CPU utilization, less starvation).

title: Batch vs multiprogramming OS
body: Batch OS groups similar jobs; multiprogramming keeps multiple jobs in memory so CPU stays busy when one job waits on I/O.

title: Multitasking vs multiprocessing
body: Multitasking: one CPU, time-sharing. Multiprocessing: multiple CPUs, better throughput and reliability.

title: RTOS vs general-purpose OS
body: Give two examples of real-time systems and why timing guarantees matter.

title: Program vs process vs thread
body: Program: compiled code on disk. Process: program in execution in RAM. Thread: lightweight execution path within a process.

title: Thread vs process context switch
body: Thread switch saves PC/registers/stack within same address space; process switch also switches memory space and flushes cache.

title: Multitasking vs multithreading
body: Compare isolation: processes have memory protection; threads in a process share memory.

title: Kernel vs user space
body: Kernel interacts with hardware; user space runs applications via system calls without direct hardware access.

title: Monolithic vs micro vs hybrid kernel
body: Monolithic: all services in kernel (fast, less modular). Micro: minimal kernel, services in user space (modular, slower IPC). Hybrid: e.g. Windows, macOS.

title: Kernel responsibilities
body: Name the four classic kernel functions: process, memory, file, and I/O management.

title: What is a system call?
body: A system call lets user programs request kernel services (files, processes, devices) via a controlled trap from user mode to kernel mode.

title: System call categories
body: List five system call types: process control, file, device, information, communication.

title: Boot sequence overview
body: Power on -> firmware (BIOS/UEFI POST) -> bootloader (MBR/EFI) -> kernel -> user space.

title: 32-bit vs 64-bit OS
body: 32-bit: ~4GB addressable per process typical limit. 64-bit: vastly larger virtual address space and can run more RAM efficiently.

title: Memory hierarchy
body: Register (fastest, smallest) -> cache -> RAM -> secondary storage (largest, slowest).

title: PCB and process creation steps
body: OS loads program, allocates stack/heap, sets up I/O, stores metadata in PCB, then starts at main().

title: Five process states
body: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated — transitions driven by scheduler and I/O.

title: Ready, job, and wait queues
body: Which scheduler picks from the job queue vs ready queue? (LTS vs STS)

title: Context switching overhead
body: Save old process state in PCB, load new process state — pure overhead, no useful work during switch.

title: Zombie vs orphan process
body: Orphan: parent died, adopted by init. Zombie: child exited but PCB entry remains until parent wait().

title: FCFS and convoy effect
body: FCFS serves first-arrived first; convoy effect: short jobs wait behind one long CPU-bound job.

title: Scheduling metrics
body: TAT = completion - arrival; WT = TAT - burst; response = first CPU time - arrival.

title: SJF preemptive vs non-preemptive
body: Non-preemptive SJF can starve long jobs. Preemptive SJF reduces average wait time but needs burst estimates.

title: Round Robin and time quantum
body: RR gives each ready process a time slice; small TQ increases context switches; large TQ approaches FCFS.

title: Priority scheduling and ageing
body: How does ageing prevent starvation in priority scheduling?

title: MLQ vs MLFQ
body: MLQ: fixed queues per process type. MLFQ: processes move between queues based on behavior (CPU vs I/O bound).

title: Benefits of multithreading
body: Responsiveness, resource sharing, economy (cheaper than new processes), multicore utilization.

title: Critical section and race condition
body: Critical section accesses shared data; race condition when outcome depends on interleaving without synchronization.

title: Mutex limitations
body: Why can a simple flag variable fail to fix races? (Not atomic; no blocking semantics.)

title: Semaphores vs mutex
body: Binary semaphore ~ mutex (one resource). Counting semaphore allows N instances of a resource.

title: Condition variables
body: Wait on a condition with a lock; avoids busy waiting; signaled when event occurs.

title: Dining philosophers problem
body: Why can five philosophers each pick up left fork and deadlock? Name one fix (ordering, limit diners, etc.).

title: Four deadlock conditions
body: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait — all must hold for deadlock.

title: Deadlock prevention vs avoidance
body: Prevention: break a necessary condition. Avoidance: banker/safe-state — grant only if system stays safe.

title: Banker algorithm idea
body: Explain safe state in one sentence: system can allocate resources so all processes can finish in some order.

title: Logical vs physical address
body: CPU generates logical (virtual) addresses; MMU maps to physical frames using base/limit or paging.

title: Internal vs external fragmentation
body: Internal: wasted space inside allocated partition. External: free holes too small to use without compaction.

title: First fit vs best fit
body: Which allocation is faster? Which tends to leave smaller leftover holes?

title: Paging basics
body: Divide logical memory into pages and physical memory into frames; page table maps page to frame; avoids external fragmentation.

title: TLB purpose
body: Translation look-aside buffer caches recent page mappings to speed address translation.

title: Segmentation vs paging
body: Segmentation matches user view (code/data/stack segments); paging is fixed-size and OS-oriented; modern systems often combine both.

title: Demand paging and page fault
body: Pages brought in only when referenced; invalid page -> trap -> OS loads page into frame -> restart instruction.

title: LRU vs FIFO page replacement
body: Why can FIFO show Belady anomaly? Why is LRU often better in practice?

title: Thrashing
body: Process faults repeatedly without making progress; working set / page-fault frequency controls can reduce thrashing.
